message.content:
Adversarially review implemented task T119 ROUND 3 (goal G7, defect D36). Return the structured verdict JSON {taskId, verdict:"approve"|"disapprove", criticism:[], questions:[], defects:[], rationale, summary} and WRITE NOTHING to the ledger.

Fetch acceptance: `mcp__ledger__fetch_item` ledger=tasks item=T119.
Branch implement/T119-r3 at commit ff18ccd036c34e98783f75b50b7629e7148ce629, based at main 36f0bd3 (worker reset from stale base; VERIFY merge-base==36f0bd3, review merge-base..HEAD). Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ab106078a231db918.
Diff: `git -C /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia diff 36f0bd3 ff18ccd036c34e98783f75b50b7629e7148ce629`.

CONTEXT: YOU found BOTH round-2 faults with executable probes: (A) permanent blackhole at anchor==window+2 when the lone in-budget seq-1 init is lost (0/499); (B) ObserveRecovered/FEC bypass of the gate re-pinning next high (2→210). Round 3 claims to fix both:
- FIX 3: pendingLowDrops counter → after O(window) consecutive pendingLow suspect-drops, fall back to plain unpin (started=false, self-heals).
- FIX 4a: admit low re-anchor clears ring/buf/waiting (mirror resync).
- FIX 4b: ObserveRecovered drops recovered frames while pendingLow armed.

RE-RUN YOUR ROUND-2 EXECUTABLE PROBES against round 3 (write throwaway tests, run, remove): (A) window=64, old boot sent enough to make anchor==window+2, RebaselineToLow, DROP the seq-1 init, then stream new-boot seqs 2..500 — assert the stream now DELIVERS (self-heals via the counter fallback) instead of 0/499; also probe anchor much larger than window+2 with seq-1 lost. (B) ObserveRecovered a recovered OLD-boot frame while armed, then the low init — assert next does NOT jump high and no stale frame is delivered. Then hunt for a NEW blackhole/stale-delivery the fixes miss: e.g. the counter resetting spuriously so it never reaches threshold; a legitimate low init arriving at drop-count==threshold-1 being abandoned; a partial ring clear leaving buf inconsistent; ObserveRecovered right as the gate clears. Also verify NO regression (round-1/2 fixes, saturated D36, stale-high race, per-peer, lock discipline, D32). Run `cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ab106078a231db918 && nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/bind/... ./internal/reseq/...' && nix develop -c sh -c 'go test -race ./internal/bind/...' && nix develop -c just lint`. Split criticism[] vs questions[]; file genuine defects. Be maximally adversarial — do not approve unless you have actively tried and failed to break the gate.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: {"item":{"id":"T119","milestoneId":"M39","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Wire the peer-restart event to the per-peer Resequencer.Rebaseline in dispatchInbound","description":"In internal/bind/multipath.go dispatchInbound's non-echo Probe branch (around the pr.reflector.Reflect(raw) call, ~:1689), consume T116's epochChanged return flag: when Reflect reports an authenticated peer-restart epoch change, re-anchor THAT peer's resequencer so the restarted peer's low-outer-seq frames (the wrapped WG init/response, outer-seq ~1) admit instead of being SUSPECT-dropped. Atomically Load the resequencer and nil-check it (absent mid-teardown, like the DATA branch; a torn-down peer re-instantiates an UNSTARTED ring that needs no rebaseline). Perform the re-anchor OUTSIDE m.mu with no other lock held — the resequencer keeps its own mutex; NEVER nest it — mirroring the SetPeerRemote→Rebaseline discipline (multipath.go ~:2167-2183). Because `pr` is the demux-resolved per-peer view, this SINGLE site covers BOTH the edge single-concentrator primary resequencer AND every concentrator per-peer resequencer, and both restart directions. Leave the existing SetPeerRemote→Rebaseline hub-failover trigger (D32) untouched.\n\nRE-PIN RACE HANDLING — REQUIRED (plan review R126, [fable]): the plain Rebaseline() clears `started` and trusts the NEXT frame to re-anchor, but under the D36 saturation precondition a stale OLD-boot HIGH-seq straggler can still be draining from carrier/modem queues and land between the rebaseline and the wrapped low-seq init, re-pinning `next` HIGH and (with once-per-epoch dedup) blocking recovery. Use a LOW-ANCHOR re-anchor instead: extend internal/reseq with a variant (e.g. Resequencer.RebaselineToLow, or a pending-low-re-anchor mode on Rebaseline) that, after unpinning, re-anchors `next` ONLY on a frame whose outer-seq is MORE THAN one window BELOW the pre-rebaseline release point (the genuine restarted-stream low-seq) — treating stale-high stragglers as ordinary SUSPECT-drops until the low init arrives, and idempotent across repeated restart signals. Keep the existing hub-failover Rebaseline() call (D32) as-is (there the old sender has been dark the whole detection window, so no stale-high race exists) OR migrate it to the same low-anchor variant if that is cleaner — planner-neutral, but do not regress the D32 test.","acceptance":"New bind regression test (internal/bind, following concentrator_peer_test.go/probe_test.go fixtures) whose comment documents it fails without this wiring (the D36 repro): advance a peer's resequencer `next` far past resequencerWindow (2048) with high-seq DATA, simulate that peer restarting (new-sessionID probes: learn challenge then adopt → epochChanged=true), then deliver a LOW outer-seq DATA frame (the wrapped init) — assert it is DELIVERED (re-anchored), Stats().Rebaselines increments, and no dropSuspect increment from it. MUST INCLUDE the STALE-HIGH race case: after the restart re-anchor, inject a stale OLD-boot HIGH-seq straggler BEFORE the low-seq init and assert it does NOT re-pin `next` high (it is SUSPECT-dropped) and the subsequent low-seq init still admits. Assert for BOTH the primary peerState AND an AddConcentratorPeer peer, and that another bound peer's resequencer is undisturbed; a same-epoch (non-restart) probe must NOT re-anchor. The existing D32 hub-failover Rebaseline test still passes. `go test ./internal/bind/... ./internal/reseq/... && go test ./...` pass; `go test -race ./internal/bind/...` clean.","suggestedModel":"frontier","dependsOn":["T116"],"ledgerRefs":["goals:G7","defects:D36"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T09:21:51.992Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:33:20.196Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

diff --git a/internal/bind/multipath.go b/internal/bind/multipath.go
index 970615c..5033493 100644
--- a/internal/bind/multipath.go
+++ b/internal/bind/multipath.go
@@ -1825,7 +1825,7 @@ func (m *Multipath) dispatchInbound(ps *peerPathState, fr frame.Frame, raw []byt
 			return
 		}
 		if pr.reflector != nil {
-			if echo, _, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
+			if echo, epochChanged, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
 				// UDP writes are goroutine-safe, so this receive-goroutine reflection
 				// races no in-flight Send on the same socket.
 				if _, werr := ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(echo, srcAP); werr == nil {
@@ -1834,6 +1834,27 @@ func (m *Multipath) dispatchInbound(ps *peerPathState, fr frame.Frame, raw []byt
 					// exactly like a DATA/PARITY write — only on a nil write error.
 					ps.txBytes.Add(uint64(len(echo)))
 				}
+				if epochChanged {
+					// Authenticated PEER RESTART (T116/T119): the reflector reports THIS
+					// peer's epoch changed — a new-sessionID adoption over an already-adopted
+					// path. The restarted peer's outer-seq resets near 1, far below the
+					// release point the pre-restart stream advanced THIS peer's resequencer's
+					// `next` to, so its wrapped WG init (outer-seq ~1) would be SUSPECT-dropped
+					// and the tunnel never re-establishes. Re-anchor via a LOW-ANCHOR
+					// re-baseline so the restarted low-seq init re-pins the release point while
+					// a stale HIGH-seq old-boot straggler still draining cannot re-pin it high
+					// (defect D36 re-pin race). Because pr is the demux-resolved per-peer view,
+					// this ONE site covers the edge single-concentrator primary AND every
+					// concentrator per-peer resequencer, both restart directions. Load the
+					// resequencer atomically and nil-check it — absent mid-teardown, like the
+					// DATA branch; a torn-down peer re-instantiates an UNSTARTED ring that needs
+					// no re-anchor. Done OUTSIDE m.mu with no other lock held (dispatchInbound
+					// runs on a readLoop that never takes m.mu; the resequencer keeps its own
+					// mutex — never nest it), mirroring the SetPeerRemote->Rebaseline discipline.
+					if rq := pr.resequencer.Load(); rq != nil {
+						rq.RebaselineToLow()
+					}
+				}
 			}
 		}
 	default:
diff --git a/internal/reseq/reseq.go b/internal/reseq/reseq.go
index 62fe1a1..0dd7cc3 100644
--- a/internal/reseq/reseq.go
+++ b/internal/reseq/reseq.go
@@ -144,6 +144,26 @@ type Resequencer struct {
 	resyncLo   uint64
 	resyncHi   uint64
 
+	// Pending low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart, T119). After RebaselineToLow the
+	// release point is UNPINNED but NOT re-anchored on the next arbitrary frame:
+	// pendingLow keeps `next` held at pendingLowAnchor (the pre-rebaseline release
+	// point) so a stale HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the OLD boot is
+	// SUSPECT-dropped, and `next` re-anchors ONLY when a genuine restarted-stream
+	// low-seq — a frame more than one window BELOW pendingLowAnchor — arrives. This
+	// closes the D36 re-pin race the plain Rebaseline leaves open (plan review R126).
+	pendingLow       bool
+	pendingLowAnchor uint64

1	package reseq
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injected time source. Production wires a monotonic wall clock
10	// (SystemClock); tests wire a hand-advanced fake so ordering, window, and
11	// timeout behaviour are deterministic with no real sleeps. It is intentionally
12	// structurally identical to telemetry.Clock so the same fake can drive both.
13	type Clock interface {
14		Now() time.Time
15	}
16	
17	// SystemClock is the production Clock backed by the monotonic wall clock.
18	type SystemClock struct{}
19	
20	// Now returns the current monotonic time.
21	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
22	
23	// Discontinuity/resync guard tuning. A DATA frame is UNAUTHENTICATED by design
24	// (internal/frame/frame.go: "DATA/PARITY forgeable by design"), so a garbage
25	// datagram decoding as KindData (p ~ 1/256) delivers a uniformly-random uint64
26	// OuterSeq, and a genuine peer PROCESS RESTART resets the peer's outerSeq counter
27	// to 1. Neither may be trusted to move the release point arbitrarily.
28	const (
29		// resyncFactor (K) bounds how far AHEAD of the release point a single
30		// unauthenticated frame is trusted to advance the window as a plausible loss
31		// burst. Under bounded cross-path reorder a legitimate frame sits within one
32		// window of next; a forward jump up to K windows is still treated as a loss
33		// burst (the sound bounded-memory window-advance handles it in O(window)), but
34		// a jump of K windows or more is SUSPECT: a single such frame never advances
35		// next. K=4 leaves generous headroom above the 1-window reorder bound while
36		// capping the release point a single trusted frame can skip to (K-1) windows.
37		resyncFactor = 4
38	
39		// resyncCorroborate (C) is how many out-of-band frames carrying DISTINCT seqs
40		// that mutually span less than one window must be observed before the release
41		// point is re-pinned. A genuine peer restart (1,2,3,...) or a consistent
42		// long-outage forward jump emits connected, distinct seqs that trivially
43		// corroborate. A repeated identical seq does NOT advance the count: a single
44		// junk or forged datagram re-delivered — a network duplicate, or a forger
45		// replaying one datagram — contributes only ONE distinct seq, so it can never
46		// self-corroborate. Corroboration therefore requires C INDEPENDENT junk seqs to
47		// land mutually within one window; each is independent in 2^64, so that occurs
48		// with probability ~(window/2^64)^(C-1) ~ 1e-32 for C=3 and window=2048 — junk
49		// never triggers a resync, while a real discontinuity resyncs after losing at
50		// most C-1 frames. Any in-band (near-current) frame resets the run, so ordinary
51		// traffic interspersed with rare junk never accumulates a false corroboration.
52		resyncCorroborate = 3
53	)
54	
55	// Item is one released inner datagram plus the outer source address of the DATA
56	// frame that carried it. The source travels with the payload so the multipath
57	// Bind can pin its single virtual endpoint to the FIRST delivered frame's
58	// source even when that frame was buffered and released out of arrival order.
59	type Item struct {
60		Payload []byte
61		Src     netip.AddrPort
62	}
63	
64	// slot is one ring cell. A live cell (occupied) holds exactly one buffered DATA
65	// frame whose OuterSeq maps to this index; seq is retained so a stale cell (an
66	// already-drained seq that shares the index modulo window) is never mistaken for
67	// a live one.
68	type slot struct {
69		seq      uint64
70		src      netip.AddrPort
71		payload  []byte
72		occupied bool
73	}
74	
75	// Resequencer is a bounded-window, timeout-based receive resequencing buffer. It
76	// consumes DATA frames tagged with the multipath codec's OWN outer sequence
77	// number (frame.Data.OuterSeq — never WireGuard's inner counter) arriving out of
78	// order across paths, and releases the decoded inner datagrams in strictly
79	// ascending outer-seq order before they reach the WireGuard engine. This absorbs
80	// multipath reorder entirely in the outer layer so WG's RFC 6479 anti-replay
81	// filter (window 8128, see docs/p0-findings.md §6) only ever sees monotonically
82	// increasing inner counters and never drops a legitimately-delivered packet.
83	//
84	// Guarantees (see the package tests):
85	//   - Ordering: released frames are strictly ascending in outer-seq. A frame
86	//     whose seq is below the release point is dropped, never delivered late, so
87	//     delivery is monotonic and WG sees no replay-window regression.
88	//   - Exactly-once: duplicates (same seq re-observed before or after release) are
89	//     dropped; every deliverable frame is released exactly once.
90	//   - Bounded memory: at most `window` frames are buffered. A frame at or beyond
91	//     next+window forces the window to advance (releasing/skipping the tail),
92	//     so an adversarial reorder/loss trace cannot grow the buffer without bound.
93	//   - Timeout progress: when the head-of-line seq is missing, buffered frames
94	//     ahead of the gap are held at most `timeout`; then the gap is skipped
95	//     (treated as lost) and the run released, rather than stalling forever. Each
96	//     distinct head-of-line gap gets its OWN full timeout.
97	//   - Bounded compute: every Observe runs in O(window). No frame — however wild
98	//     its (unauthenticated, possibly forged) seq — can make the release point
99	//     advance one seq at a time across a gap of up to 2^64 under the mutex.
100	//   - Discontinuity resilience: an unauthenticated frame whose seq lies farther
101	//     than resyncFactor*window from next never moves the release point on its own.
102	//     A genuine peer restart or long outage — a run of frames carrying DISTINCT
103	//     seqs that mutually fall within one window — re-pins (resyncs) the release
104	//     point within a bounded number of frames; uniformly-random junk seqs, and a
105	//     single junk/forged seq re-delivered any number of times, do not.
106	//
107	// Concurrency: every method is guarded by one mutex, so the shared instance is
108	// safe for the multipath Bind's per-path receive goroutines. The mutex is held
109	// only for in-memory bookkeeping — never across a syscall — so it does not
110	// perturb the Bind's lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path or its
111	// syscall-outside-mutex send discipline.
112	//
113	// FEC seam (T24): Observe is the sole ingestion point and is keyed purely by
114	// outer-seq. An FEC decoder inserted on the receive path recovers missing DATA
115	// frames from PARITY and feeds each recovered frame's ORIGINAL outer-seq through
116	// Observe, identically to a natively-received frame — so FEC recovery slots in
117	// BEFORE resequencing with no change to this type.
118	type Resequencer struct {
119		window  uint64
120		timeout time.Duration
121		clock   Clock
122	
123		mu      sync.Mutex
124		started bool   // false until the first Observe pins the initial next
125		next    uint64 // lowest outer-seq not yet released
126		ring    []slot // len == window; indexed by seq % window
127		buf     int    // number of occupied slots
128	
129		ready    []Item // FIFO of released items awaiting Pop
130		readyPos int    // read cursor into ready (front of the FIFO)
131	
132		// Head-of-line timeout: when next is a gap but frames ahead are buffered,
133		// waiting is armed with a deadline measured from when the gap first formed.
134		waiting  bool
135		deadline time.Time
136	
137		// Discontinuity/resync guard: the current run of out-of-band (suspect) frames.
138		// resyncSeqs holds the DISTINCT suspect seqs seen in the run (a repeated
139		// identical seq does not advance corroboration); [resyncLo, resyncHi] is the
140		// run's seq span. When resyncSeqs reaches resyncCorroborate distinct seqs the
141		// run is deemed a real discontinuity and the release point re-pins (see
142		// tryResync). It holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so memory stays bounded.
143		resyncSeqs []uint64
144		resyncLo   uint64
145		resyncHi   uint64
146	
147		// Pending low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart, T119). After RebaselineToLow the
148		// release point is UNPINNED but NOT re-anchored on the next arbitrary frame:
149		// pendingLow keeps `next` held at pendingLowAnchor (the pre-rebaseline release
150		// point) so a stale HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the OLD boot is
151		// SUSPECT-dropped, and `next` re-anchors ONLY when a genuine restarted-stream
152		// low-seq — a frame more than one window BELOW pendingLowAnchor — arrives. This
153		// closes the D36 re-pin race the plain Rebaseline leaves open (plan review R126).
154		pendingLow       bool
155		pendingLowAnchor uint64
156	
157		// pendingLowDrops counts consecutive SUSPECT drops taken while the low-anchor gate
158		// is armed. It BOUNDS the gate so it can never permanently blackhole (round-3 FIX 3,
159		// review R150): at anchor == window+2 the only in-budget re-anchor frame is seq 1, so
160		// if that lone wrapped-init frame is LOST every later new-boot frame fails
161		// `anchor - seq > window` and would be SUSPECT-dropped forever. After O(window) such
162		// drops the gate FALLS BACK to a plain unpin (started=false), which self-heals via the
163		// existing resync-corroboration path. Reset to 0 whenever the gate is (re-)armed or
164		// cleared.
165		pendingLowDrops uint64
166	
167		// Diagnostics (read via the accessors; useful for the bounded-memory asserts).
168		highWater   int    // max occupied slots ever held
169		dropDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
170		dropLate    uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
171		dropSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
172		skipped     uint64 // seqs skipped (treated lost) by window-advance or timeout
173		releasedN   uint64 // frames released for delivery
174		resyncs     uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
175		rebaselines uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
176	}
177	
178	// New returns a resequencer buffering at most window outer-seq positions and
179	// holding a head-of-line-blocked run for at most timeout before skipping the gap.
180	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
181	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
182	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
183		if window == 0 {
184			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
185		}
186		if clock == nil {
187			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
188		}
189		return &Resequencer{
190			window:  window,
191			timeout: timeout,
192			clock:   clock,
193			ring:    make([]slot, window),
194		}
195	}
196	
197	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
198	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
199	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
200	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
201	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
202	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
203		now := r.clock.Now()
204		r.mu.Lock()
205		defer r.mu.Unlock()
206	
207		if !r.started {
208			r.started = true
209			r.next = seq
210		}
211	
212		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
213		// where this frame lands.
214		r.expire(now)
215	
216		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
217		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
218		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
219		if !r.admit(seq) {
220			return
221		}
222	
223		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
224		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
225			r.dropDup++
226			return
227		}
228		cell.seq = seq
229		cell.src = src
230		cell.payload = payload
231		cell.occupied = true
232		r.buf++
233		if r.buf > r.highWater {
234			r.highWater = r.buf
235		}
236	
237		r.drain()
238		r.arm(now)
239	}
240	
241	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
242	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
243	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
244	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
245	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
246	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
247	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
248	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
249	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
250	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
251	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
252		now := r.clock.Now()
253		r.mu.Lock()
254		defer r.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		if r.pendingLow {
257			// A low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart) is armed: a parity-RECOVERED frame is
258			// by definition part of the PRE-restart stream — the gate is up precisely because
259			// a restart re-anchor is pending, and a frame recoverable from the old boot's
260			// parity belongs to that old boot. Seating it would place a stale old-boot cell in
261			// [anchor, anchor+window) that keeps the head-of-line timeout live and lets
262			// expire() jump `next` high past the restarted stream (round-3 FIX 4b, review
263			// R150). ObserveRecovered otherwise bypasses admit entirely, so without this it
264			// never consults the gate. Drop it — FEC recovery must not seat state while the
265			// gate arbitrates the restart. Returns false so the /metrics recovered counter
266			// stays honest (nothing was delivered).
267			r.dropSuspect++
268			return false
269		}
270	
271		if !r.started {
272			r.started = true
273			r.next = seq
274		}
275		r.expire(now)
276	
277		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
278		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
279		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
280		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
281		// touches next or the corroboration run.
282		if seq < r.next {
283			if r.next-seq > r.window {
284				r.dropSuspect++
285			} else {
286				r.dropLate++
287			}
288			return false
289		}
290		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
291			r.dropLate++
292			return false
293		}
294	
295		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
296		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
297			r.dropDup++
298			return false // already buffered (a received frame or a prior recovery)
299		}
300		cell.seq = seq
301		cell.src = src
302		cell.payload = payload
303		cell.occupied = true
304		r.buf++
305		if r.buf > r.highWater {
306			r.highWater = r.buf
307		}
308	
309		r.drain()
310		r.arm(now)
311		return true
312	}
313	
314	// admit classifies seq against the release window and the discontinuity guard and
315	// decides whether the frame should be buffered. It returns true when seq now lies
316	// inside [next, next+window) and should be placed; false (having bumped the
317	// appropriate drop counter) when the frame is dropped. It may advance next by a
318	// bounded amount on a plausible loss burst, or re-pin next on a corroborated
319	// discontinuity. Caller holds r.mu.
320	func (r *Resequencer) admit(seq uint64) bool {
321		if r.pendingLow {
322			// A low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart, T119) is in effect: re-anchor the
323			// release point ONLY on the genuine restarted-stream low-seq — a frame more
324			// than one window BELOW the pre-rebaseline release point. A stale HIGH-seq
325			// straggler still draining from the OLD boot (at or near the old release
326			// point) is an ordinary SUSPECT-drop and must NOT re-pin `next` high, so
327			// recovery is not blocked once-per-epoch (defect D36 re-pin race). The
328			// seq < anchor guard is load-bearing: without it a straggler at or above the
329			// anchor would underflow the unsigned subtraction and spuriously re-anchor.
330			if seq < r.pendingLowAnchor && r.pendingLowAnchor-seq > r.window {
331				r.pendingLow = false
332				r.pendingLowDrops = 0
333				r.resyncReset()
334				// CLEAR the ring/buf on re-anchor (mirror resync, round-3 FIX 4a, review
335				// R150): a stale occupied cell left from the OLD boot — e.g. a parity-
336				// recovered old-boot frame seated in [anchor, anchor+window) — must NOT
337				// survive the re-anchor to next=seq. If it did, its cell would keep the
338				// head-of-line timeout live and expire() would jump `next` high past the
339				// restarted stream, suspect-dropping the new boot and delivering the stale
340				// frame; a surviving occupied cell also corrupts buf accounting on the next
341				// same-index placement (occupied-cell overwrite still does buf++).
342				for i := range r.ring {
343					r.ring[i] = slot{}
344				}
345				r.buf = 0
346				r.waiting = false
347				r.next = seq
348				return true
349			}
350			// BOUND the gate so a LOST re-anchor init frame can never permanently blackhole
351			// the restarted stream (round-3 FIX 3, review R150). After O(window) consecutive
352			// pendingLow suspect-drops, FALL BACK to a plain unpin (started=false): the next
353			// Observe re-anchors `next` and the existing resync-corroboration guard self-heals
354			// thereafter, exactly as the D32 hub-failover Rebaseline does — bounded loss
355			// instead of a permanent blackhole.
356			r.pendingLowDrops++
357			if r.pendingLowDrops > r.window {
358				r.pendingLow = false
359				r.pendingLowDrops = 0
360				r.started = false
361				r.resyncReset()
362			}
363			r.dropSuspect++
364			return false
365		}
366		if seq < r.next {
367			// Below the release point. A frame more than one window below next is
368			// impossibly late under bounded reorder — treat it as SUSPECT (a peer
369			// restart resets outerSeq to 1, so its frames land here). A frame within a
370			// window below next is an ordinary straggler/duplicate.
371			if r.next-seq > r.window {
372				if r.tryResync(seq) {
373					return true
374				}
375				r.dropSuspect++
376				return false
377			}
378			r.resyncReset() // near-current traffic: not a discontinuity
379			r.dropLate++
380			return false
381		}
382		// seq >= next.
383		if seq-r.next >= resyncFactor*r.window {
384			// Too far ahead to be a plausible loss burst — SUSPECT (garbage-decoded or
385			// forged high seq). A single such frame must not advance next.
386			if r.tryResync(seq) {
387				return true
388			}
389			r.dropSuspect++
390			return false
391		}
392		// In-window, or a moderate (plausible loss-burst) forward jump: legitimate
393		// near-current traffic, so any in-progress discontinuity run is broken.
394		r.resyncReset()
395		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
396			// Beyond the window: advance the release point so the frame fits at the top
397			// of the window, releasing buffered frames below the new base in order and
398			// skipping the (assumed lost) gaps. This is what bounds memory.
399			r.advanceTo(seq - r.window + 1)
400		}
401		return true
402	}
403	
404	// Pop returns the next in-order released inner datagram, or ok=false when none is
405	// ready. It also advances the head-of-line timeout, so a receive goroutine that
406	// only drains still makes timeout progress.
407	func (r *Resequencer) Pop() (Item, bool) {
408		now := r.clock.Now()
409		r.mu.Lock()
410		defer r.mu.Unlock()
411	
412		r.expire(now)
413	
414		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
415			return Item{}, false
416		}
417		it := r.ready[r.readyPos]
418		r.ready[r.readyPos] = Item{} // release the payload reference
419		r.readyPos++
420		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
421			// FIFO drained: reset to reuse the backing array, keeping it bounded.
422			r.ready = r.ready[:0]
423			r.readyPos = 0
424		}
425		return it, true
426	}
427	
428	// drain releases the contiguous run starting at next into the ready FIFO. Caller
429	// holds r.mu.
430	func (r *Resequencer) drain() {
431		for {
432			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
433			if !cell.occupied || cell.seq != r.next {
434				return
435			}
436			r.release(cell)
437			r.next++
438		}
439	}
440	
441	// advanceTo moves next up to target, releasing any occupied cell it passes (in
442	// ascending order) and counting the empty positions as skipped (lost) seqs. It is
443	// O(window), NEVER O(target-next): all occupied cells live in [next, next+window),
444	// so once next reaches next+window the remaining gap is provably empty and is
445	// closed by arithmetic rather than iterated. Caller holds r.mu; target must be
446	// > next.
447	func (r *Resequencer) advanceTo(target uint64) {
448		// Only [next, next+window) can hold occupied cells; iterate at most that far.
449		limit := target
450		if limit > r.next+r.window {
451			limit = r.next + r.window
452		}
453		for r.next < limit {
454			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
455			if cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next {
456				r.release(cell)
457			} else {
458				r.skipped++
459			}
460			r.next++
461		}
462		if r.next < target {
463			// The remaining gap [next, target) is entirely empty — no occupied cell can
464			// live a full window ahead of the old release point. Close it by arithmetic.
465			r.skipped += target - r.next
466			r.next = target
467		}
468	}
469	
470	// release moves one occupied cell's payload into the ready FIFO and clears it.
471	// Caller holds r.mu.
472	func (r *Resequencer) release(cell *slot) {
473		r.ready = append(r.ready, Item{Payload: cell.payload, Src: cell.src})
474		cell.occupied = false
475		cell.payload = nil
476		r.buf--
477		r.releasedN++
478	}
479	
480	// expire skips a head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed: it jumps next to
481	// the smallest buffered seq (treating the intervening seqs as lost) and releases
482	// the now-contiguous run. Caller holds r.mu.
483	func (r *Resequencer) expire(now time.Time) {
484		if !r.waiting || now.Before(r.deadline) {
485			return
486		}
487		minSeq, ok := r.smallestBuffered()
488		if !ok {
489			r.waiting = false
490			return
491		}
492		// Skip the lost gap [next, minSeq), then release from minSeq onward.
493		for r.next < minSeq {
494			r.skipped++
495			r.next++
496		}
497		r.drain()
498		// Clear the armed state BEFORE re-arming so that a distinct SECOND gap exposed
499		// by this release gets its own full timeout rather than inheriting this gap's
500		// already-elapsed deadline (which would skip it with ~zero hold).
501		r.waiting = false
502		r.arm(now)
503	}
504	
505	// arm (re)evaluates the head-of-line timeout after a change. When next is a gap
506	// but buffered frames sit ahead of it, it starts the hold clock (only if not
507	// already running, so the deadline measures from when the gap first formed).
508	// Otherwise it disarms. Caller holds r.mu.
509	func (r *Resequencer) arm(now time.Time) {
510		cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
511		headPresent := cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next
512		if !headPresent && r.buf > 0 {
513			if !r.waiting {
514				r.waiting = true
515				r.deadline = now.Add(r.timeout)
516			}
517		} else {
518			r.waiting = false
519		}
520	}
521	
522	// tryResync feeds one out-of-band (suspect) seq into the discontinuity guard. It
523	// re-pins the release point ONLY after resyncCorroborate DISTINCT suspect seqs
524	// whose values mutually span less than one window (a genuine peer restart or long
525	// outage emits connected, distinct seqs that corroborate; uniformly-random junk
526	// seqs, each independent in 2^64, do not, and a single junk/forged seq re-delivered
527	// contributes only ONE distinct value so it can never self-corroborate). It
528	// returns true and performs the resync when the corroboration threshold is met —
529	// re-pinning next to the triggering seq so the caller places it as the new head
530	// and delivery resumes immediately, without a phantom gap or an extra timeout —
531	// and false while still collecting or when the seq fails to corroborate the
532	// current run. Caller holds r.mu.
533	func (r *Resequencer) tryResync(seq uint64) bool {
534		if len(r.resyncSeqs) == 0 || r.spanExceeds(seq) {
535			// Start (or restart) the run at this seq: it does not corroborate the
536			// current run, so it becomes the anchor of a fresh one.
537			r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi = seq, seq
538			r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs[:0], seq)
539			return false
540		}
541		if r.runContains(seq) {
542			// A repeated identical seq does NOT advance corroboration: a lone junk or
543			// forged datagram re-delivered must not count as independent corroboration.
544			return false
545		}
546		if seq < r.resyncLo {
547			r.resyncLo = seq
548		}
549		if seq > r.resyncHi {
550			r.resyncHi = seq
551		}
552		r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs, seq)
553		if len(r.resyncSeqs) < resyncCorroborate {
554			return false
555		}
556		// Corroborated discontinuity: re-pin to the triggering seq and resume delivery.
557		r.resync(seq)
558		return true
559	}
560	
561	// runContains reports whether seq is already one of the distinct suspect seqs in
562	// the current corroboration run. The run holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so
563	// this scan is O(C). Caller holds r.mu.
564	func (r *Resequencer) runContains(seq uint64) bool {
565		for _, s := range r.resyncSeqs {
566			if s == seq {
567				return true
568			}
569		}
570		return false
571	}
572	
573	// spanExceeds reports whether adding seq to the current corroboration run would
574	// make its seq span reach or exceed one window (i.e. seq does not mutually fall
575	// within one window of the run so far). Caller holds r.mu.
576	func (r *Resequencer) spanExceeds(seq uint64) bool {
577		lo, hi := r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi
578		if seq < lo {
579			lo = seq
580		}
581		if seq > hi {
582			hi = seq
583		}
584		return hi-lo >= r.window
585	}
586	
587	// resync re-pins the release point to base after a confirmed discontinuity,
588	// discarding all buffered frames (they belong to the pre-discontinuity stream) in
589	// O(window). The already-released FIFO is untouched — those were legitimate prior
590	// deliveries. Caller holds r.mu.
591	func (r *Resequencer) resync(base uint64) {
592		for i := range r.ring {
593			r.ring[i] = slot{}
594		}
595		r.buf = 0
596		r.next = base
597		r.waiting = false
598		r.resyncReset()
599		r.resyncs++
600	}
601	
602	// resyncReset abandons the current corroboration run. Caller holds r.mu.
603	func (r *Resequencer) resyncReset() {
604		r.resyncSeqs = r.resyncSeqs[:0]
605		r.resyncLo = 0
606		r.resyncHi = 0
607	}
608	
609	// Rebaseline discards the buffered (pre-switch) frames and UNPINS the release
610	// point so the NEXT Observe re-anchors `next` to that frame's outer-seq, exactly as
611	// if the stream had just started. It exists for the edge-side HUB FAILOVER (T57): a
612	// concentrator switch changes the DATA-frame SENDER, and the standby is a separate
613	// process whose outer-seq restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
614	// hub's high-rate stream advanced `next` to. The unauthenticated tryResync path
615	// cannot rescue this in time: it needs resyncCorroborate (3) distinct low seqs
616	// within one window, but a freshly re-handshaking standby emits only ~1 DATA frame
617	// (the handshake response) per RekeyTimeout, so corroboration falls outside the
618	// failover window and the response is dropped as SUSPECT — the tunnel never
619	// re-establishes (defect D32). Unlike a DATA frame (forgeable by design, hence the
620	// corroboration guard), THIS re-pin is driven by a TRUSTED control event — the
621	// operator-configured ordered-endpoint switch, above the unauthenticated wire — so
622	// re-anchoring on the next single frame is sound. Buffered frames (from the dead
623	// prior hub) are discarded in O(window); the already-released FIFO of prior
624	// legitimate deliveries is untouched. Idempotent and safe before the first Observe
625	// (started stays false). Takes r.mu; the caller must NOT hold it.
626	func (r *Resequencer) Rebaseline() {
627		r.mu.Lock()
628		defer r.mu.Unlock()
629		for i := range r.ring {
630			r.ring[i] = slot{}
631		}
632		r.buf = 0
633		r.started = false
634		r.waiting = false
635		// A full unpin SUPERSEDES any pending low-anchor re-baseline (T119). If a peer
636		// restart had armed pendingLow (via RebaselineToLow) and the restarted low-seq
637		// init had not yet re-anchored `next`, a subsequent D32 hub failover must NOT
638		// leave the stale pendingLow gate in force: admit would keep classifying every
639		// fail-back frame against the now-stale pendingLowAnchor and blackhole the
640		// standby's stream, violating this method's documented "next Observe re-anchors
641		// next" postcondition. Clearing it restores the plain unpin-and-trust-next path.
642		r.pendingLow = false
643		r.pendingLowDrops = 0
644		r.resyncReset()
645		r.rebaselines++
646	}
647	
648	// RebaselineToLow re-baselines the release point for a PEER RESTART (T119) using a
649	// LOW-ANCHOR rule the plain Rebaseline lacks. Like Rebaseline it discards the
650	// buffered pre-restart frames in O(window) and leaves the already-released FIFO
651	// untouched, but instead of unpinning `next` and trusting the NEXT frame to
652	// re-anchor, it PINS a pending low-anchor at the current release point: `next`
653	// re-anchors ONLY when a frame more than one window BELOW that point arrives — the
654	// genuine restarted-stream low-seq (the wrapped WG init, outer-seq ~1). Any stale
655	// HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the OLD boot lands at or near the old
656	// release point and is SUSPECT-dropped, so it can never re-pin `next` high and
657	// block recovery (the D36 re-pin race under the saturation precondition that a
658	// plain Rebaseline — which re-anchors on the first frame, high straggler included —
659	// cannot survive; plan review R126). Idempotent across repeated restart signals: a
660	// second call while a re-anchor is still pending keeps the ORIGINAL (high) anchor.
661	// Safe before the first Observe (an UNSTARTED ring needs no re-anchor — the first
662	// Observe pins next normally).
663	//
664	// The low-anchor gate is armed ONLY when the pre-rebaseline release point is high
665	// enough for it to be satisfiable: the re-anchor predicate is
666	// `seq < pendingLowAnchor && pendingLowAnchor - seq > window`, and the restarted
667	// sender's first DATA is outer-seq ~1, so it can only re-anchor when
668	// pendingLowAnchor >= window+2 (i.e. next > window+1). For a SMALL anchor
669	// (next <= window+1) the predicate is UNSATISFIABLE for any low seq — nothing would
670	// ever clear pendingLow and every subsequent frame would be SUSPECT-dropped forever
671	// (a permanent blackhole; the pre-T119 code self-heals here via resync corroboration,
672	// so pinning would be a regression). This is the realistic light-traffic / early-
673	// restart / crash-loop case. So when the anchor is small we fall back to a PLAIN
674	// unpin (started=false), re-anchoring on the next frame exactly as the D32
675	// Rebaseline does — bounded, self-healing loss instead of a blackhole. Takes r.mu;
676	// the caller must NOT hold it.
677	//
678	// Even a correctly-armed gate (anchor >= window+2) is BOUNDED so LOSS cannot blackhole
679	// it (round-3 FIX 3): the sole in-budget re-anchor frame at anchor == window+2 is
680	// outer-seq 1, and if that lone init frame is lost every later new-boot frame fails the
681	// predicate; admit therefore counts consecutive pending-low suspect-drops and, after
682	// O(window) of them, falls back to a plain unpin that self-heals via resync corroboration.
683	func (r *Resequencer) RebaselineToLow() {
684		r.mu.Lock()
685		defer r.mu.Unlock()
686		for i := range r.ring {
687			r.ring[i] = slot{}
688		}
689		r.buf = 0
690		r.waiting = false
691		r.resyncReset()
692		r.rebaselines++
693		switch {
694		case r.pendingLow:
695			// Already pending from an earlier restart signal this epoch: keep the ORIGINAL
696			// (high) anchor — idempotent under repeated restart signals for the same epoch.
697		case r.started && r.next > r.window+1:
698			// The release point is high enough for the low-anchor gate to be satisfiable:
699			// hold `next` at the pre-rebaseline release point so stale-high stragglers
700			// SUSPECT-drop until the restarted low-seq re-anchors it.
701			r.pendingLow = true
702			r.pendingLowAnchor = r.next
703			r.pendingLowDrops = 0
704		default:
705			// UNSTARTED ring, or a small release point (next <= window+1) where a low-anchor
706			// gate would be UNSATISFIABLE and permanently blackhole the restarted stream.
707			// Fall back to a plain unpin: the next Observe re-anchors next, self-healing
708			// exactly as the D32 hub-failover Rebaseline does. On an unstarted ring this is
709			// a no-op (started is already false).
710			r.started = false
711		}
712	}
713	
714	// smallestBuffered scans the ring for the lowest occupied seq at or above next.
715	// All occupied seqs lie in [next, next+window), so this is bounded by window and
716	// runs only on the (rare) timeout path. Caller holds r.mu.
717	func (r *Resequencer) smallestBuffered() (uint64, bool) {
718		best := uint64(0)
719		found := false
720		for i := range r.ring {
721			c := &r.ring[i]
722			if c.occupied && c.seq >= r.next && (!found || c.seq < best) {
723				best = c.seq
724				found = true
725			}
726		}
727		return best, found
728	}
729	
730	// Buffered reports the number of frames currently held (not yet released). It is
731	// always <= window, which the bounded-memory tests assert.
732	func (r *Resequencer) Buffered() int {
733		r.mu.Lock()
734		defer r.mu.Unlock()
735		return r.buf
736	}
737	
738	// Pending reports the number of released-but-not-yet-popped items in the FIFO.
739	func (r *Resequencer) Pending() int {
740		r.mu.Lock()
741		defer r.mu.Unlock()
742		return len(r.ready) - r.readyPos
743	}
744	
745	// HighWater reports the maximum number of frames ever buffered simultaneously.
746	func (r *Resequencer) HighWater() int {
747		r.mu.Lock()
748		defer r.mu.Unlock()
749		return r.highWater
750	}
751	
752	// Stats is a snapshot of the resequencer's cumulative counters.
753	type Stats struct {
754		Released       uint64 // frames released for delivery
755		DroppedDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
756		DroppedOld     uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
757		DroppedSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
758		Skipped        uint64 // seqs skipped (lost) by window-advance or timeout
759		Resyncs        uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
760		Rebaselines    uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
761	}
762	
763	// Stats returns a snapshot of the cumulative counters.
764	func (r *Resequencer) Stats() Stats {
765		r.mu.Lock()
766		defer r.mu.Unlock()
767		return Stats{
768			Released:       r.releasedN,
769			DroppedDup:     r.dropDup,
770			DroppedOld:     r.dropLate,
771			DroppedSuspect: r.dropSuspect,
772			Skipped:        r.skipped,
773			Resyncs:        r.resyncs,
774			Rebaselines:    r.rebaselines,
775		}
776	}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"net"
5		"net/netip"
6		"testing"
7		"time"
8	
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
11	)
12	
13	// The two sessionIDs a "restart" spans: the pre-restart boot and the post-restart boot.
14	// Distinct values make the second adoption an epoch change (a new-sessionID adoption over
15	// an already-adopted path), which is exactly what the reflector reports as epochChanged.
16	const (
17		preRestartSession  uint64 = 0xAAAA0000AAAA0001
18		postRestartSession uint64 = 0xBBBB0000BBBB0002
19		// restartHighSeq is a release point far past resequencerWindow (2048): the pre-restart
20		// boot's busy stream advanced `next` here, so the restarted boot's low outer-seq init is
21		// >1 window below it — the SUSPECT region that, before T119, blackholed the wrapped init.
22		restartHighSeq uint64 = 9000
23	)
24	
25	// deliverDATA encodes one DATA frame under psk and pushes it through the FULL receive path
26	// (handleInbound → dispatchInbound's DATA branch → the peer's resequencer), exactly as a
27	// wire datagram would arrive on view. It exercises the per-peer routing rather than poking
28	// the resequencer directly, so the assertions read the resequencer the wiring actually feeds.
29	func deliverDATA(t testing.TB, m *Multipath, view *peerPathState, psk config.Key, seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
30		t.Helper()
31		codec, err := frame.NewCodec(psk)
32		if err != nil {
33			t.Fatalf("build data codec: %v", err)
34		}
35		raw, err := codec.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: view.id, Payload: payload})
36		if err != nil {
37			t.Fatalf("encode data seq %d: %v", seq, err)
38		}
39		m.handleInbound(view, raw, src)
40	}
41	
42	// reflectProbeIssuedChallenge drives ONE inbound PROBE (encoded under psk) through
43	// handleInbound → dispatchInbound's non-echo Probe branch — the reflector adopt/restart
44	// path plus the T119 epochChanged→RebaselineToLow wiring — and returns the issued challenge
45	// carried in the reflected echo (read off the raw peer socket). Sending the reflector's own
46	// live challenge back on the NEXT probe is what makes it adopt (the responder-contributed-
47	// challenge protocol), so a caller learns the challenge here and echoes it next.
48	func reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t testing.TB, m *Multipath, view *peerPathState, psk config.Key, peer *net.UDPConn, peerAP netip.AddrPort, sessionID, probeSeq, echoedChallenge uint64) uint64 {
49		t.Helper()
50		raw, err := frame.Encode(psk, frame.Probe{
51			PathID:         view.id,
52			ProbeSeq:       probeSeq,
53			TimestampNanos: time.Now().UnixNano(),
54			SessionID:      sessionID,
55			Challenge:      echoedChallenge,
56		})
57		if err != nil {
58			t.Fatalf("encode probe (session %#x seq %d): %v", sessionID, probeSeq, err)
59		}
60		m.handleInbound(view, raw, peerAP)
61		codec, err := frame.NewCodec(psk)
62		if err != nil {
63			t.Fatalf("build echo codec: %v", err)
64		}
65		echo := readProbe(t, peer, codec)
66		return echo.Challenge
67	}
68	
69	// runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario is the shared body of the T119 acceptance. It drives one
70	// peer through: bootstrap adoption (no re-baseline), a busy stream advancing `next` far past
71	// one window, an authenticated RESTART (new-sessionID adopt → epochChanged=true → the wiring
72	// low-anchor re-baselines THIS peer's resequencer), a STALE-HIGH old-boot straggler that must
73	// NOT re-pin `next` high, the wrapped LOW-seq init that must then admit, and a same-epoch probe
74	// that must NOT re-anchor — all while a second bound peer (witnessPeer) is left untouched.
75	//
76	// It documents the failing-without-the-wiring contract: with the epochChanged→RebaselineToLow
77	// call removed, the restart leaves `next` pinned high, Rebaselines stays 0, and the wrapped
78	// low-seq init is SUSPECT-dropped — the delivery assertion below fails (the D36 repro).
79	func runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario(
80		t *testing.T,
81		m *Multipath,
82		targetView *peerPathState, targetPeer *peerState, targetPSK config.Key,
83		witnessView *peerPathState, witnessPeer *peerState, witnessPSK config.Key,
84	) {
85		t.Helper()
86		peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
87	
88		rq := targetPeer.resequencer.Load()
89		wrq := witnessPeer.resequencer.Load()
90		if rq == nil || wrq == nil {
91			t.Fatalf("resequencer not instantiated: target=%v witness=%v", rq != nil, wrq != nil)
92		}
93	
94		dataSrc := netip.MustParseAddrPort("203.0.113.9:51820")
95		lowSrc := netip.MustParseAddrPort("198.51.100.4:51820")
96	
97		// --- Bootstrap adoption (session preRestartSession): learn challenge, then adopt. A
98		//     first-ever adoption is NOT a restart, so it must NOT re-baseline. ---
99		c := reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, preRestartSession, 0, 0)
100		_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, preRestartSession, 1, c)
101		if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 0 {
102			t.Fatalf("bootstrap adoption re-baselined the resequencer: Rebaselines=%d, want 0", got)
103		}
104	
105		// --- The pre-restart boot's busy stream advances BOTH peers' release point far past one
106		//     window (via the real DATA receive path). ---
107		deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, restartHighSeq, []byte("boot-high"), dataSrc)
108		if _, ok := rq.Pop(); !ok {
109			t.Fatalf("high boot seq %d not delivered — release point did not advance", restartHighSeq)
110		}
111		deliverDATA(t, m, witnessView, witnessPSK, restartHighSeq, []byte("witness-high"), dataSrc)
112		if _, ok := wrq.Pop(); !ok {
113			t.Fatalf("witness high boot seq not delivered — its release point did not advance")
114		}
115	
116		// --- Authenticated PEER RESTART (session postRestartSession): learn the live challenge,
117		//     then adopt with it. This adoption is over an ALREADY-adopted path under a DIFFERENT
118		//     session → epochChanged=true → the T119 wiring low-anchor re-baselines THIS peer. ---
119		c2 := reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 0, 0)
120		_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 1, c2)
121		if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 1 {
122			t.Fatalf("peer restart did NOT re-baseline the target resequencer: Rebaselines=%d, want 1 "+
123				"(the epochChanged→RebaselineToLow wiring is the code under test)", got)
124		}
125	
126		// --- STALE-HIGH RACE: a stale OLD-boot high-seq straggler still draining from carrier/modem
127		//     queues lands BEFORE the low init. It must be SUSPECT-dropped and must NOT re-pin `next`
128		//     high (which a plain unpin-and-trust-next re-baseline would allow, blocking recovery). ---
129		beforeSuspect := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
130		deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, restartHighSeq+50, []byte("stale-high-straggler"), dataSrc)
131		if it, ok := rq.Pop(); ok {
132			t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler was DELIVERED (%q) — it re-pinned next high (D36 race not closed)", it.Payload)
133		}
134		if rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect <= beforeSuspect {
135			t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler not SUSPECT-dropped: DroppedSuspect stayed %d", beforeSuspect)
136		}
137	
138		// --- The wrapped WG init (restarted stream, outer-seq ~1) now admits: it is >1 window below
139		//     the pre-rebaseline release point, so it re-anchors and DELIVERS, and it must NOT itself
140		//     count as a suspect drop. ---
141		suspectBeforeLow := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
142		deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, 1, []byte("wrapped-wg-init"), lowSrc)
143		it, ok := rq.Pop()
144		if !ok || string(it.Payload) != "wrapped-wg-init" {
145			t.Fatalf("wrapped low-seq init NOT delivered after restart re-anchor: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
146		}
147		if got := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect; got != suspectBeforeLow {
148			t.Fatalf("the low-seq init was counted as a suspect drop: DroppedSuspect %d → %d", suspectBeforeLow, got)
149		}
150	
151		// --- A SAME-epoch (non-restart) probe must NOT re-anchor: a within-session probe reports
152		//     epochChanged=false, so the wiring leaves the release point alone. ---
153		rebBefore := rq.Stats().Rebaselines
154		_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 2, 0)
155		if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != rebBefore {
156			t.Fatalf("a same-epoch (non-restart) probe re-baselined: Rebaselines %d → %d", rebBefore, got)
157		}
158	
159		// --- The other bound peer's resequencer is UNDISTURBED by the target's restart. ---
160		if got := wrq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 0 {
161			t.Fatalf("witness peer re-baselined by the target's restart: Rebaselines=%d, want 0", got)
162		}
163		wSuspectBefore := wrq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
164		deliverDATA(t, m, witnessView, witnessPSK, 1, []byte("witness-should-drop"), lowSrc)
165		if it, ok := wrq.Pop(); ok {
166			t.Fatalf("witness delivered a low seq (%q) — its release point was disturbed by the target's restart", it.Payload)
167		}
168		if got := wrq.Stats().DroppedSuspect; got <= wSuspectBefore {
169			t.Fatalf("witness did not reject the low seq as suspect (its next moved): DroppedSuspect stayed %d", wSuspectBefore)
170		}
171	}
172	
173	// TestPeerRestartRebaselinesPrimaryResequencer restarts the PRIMARY (edge single-concentrator)
174	// peer and asserts the wrapped low-seq init re-anchors while a second bound peer is untouched.
175	func TestPeerRestartRebaselinesPrimaryResequencer(t *testing.T) {
176		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
177		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
178		m, beta := newConcentratorPairForRestart(t, pskA, pskB)
179	
180		primary := m.peerState
181		runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario(t, m,
182			m.paths[0], primary, pskA,
183			peerPathByName(beta, "a"), beta, pskB)
184	}
185	
186	// TestPeerRestartRebaselinesConcentratorPeerResequencer restarts an AddConcentratorPeer peer
187	// and asserts the SAME single wiring site re-anchors its per-peer resequencer, leaving the
188	// primary untouched — proving the demux-resolved per-peer view covers concentrator peers too.
189	func TestPeerRestartRebaselinesConcentratorPeerResequencer(t *testing.T) {
190		pskA := testKey(t, 0x33)
191		pskB := testKey(t, 0x44)
192		m, beta := newConcentratorPairForRestart(t, pskA, pskB)
193	
194		primary := m.peerState
195		runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario(t, m,
196			peerPathByName(beta, "a"), beta, pskB,
197			m.paths[0], primary, pskA)
198	}
199	
200	// newConcentratorPairForRestart builds an Open 2-peer concentrator over one shared socket: the
201	// primary keyed on pskA and a beta peer registered via AddConcentratorPeer keyed on pskB, with
202	// beta's heavy receive datapath (its resequencer) instantiated so a test can drive DATA at it
203	// directly rather than waiting for the demux to lazily bind its first PROBE.
204	func newConcentratorPairForRestart(t *testing.T, pskA, pskB config.Key) (*Multipath, *peerState) {
205		t.Helper()
206		clk := newFakeClock()
207		paths := loopbackPaths(1) // one shared socket, path "a"
208		m, _, _ := newProbingMultipath(t, paths, pskA, clk)
209	
210		betaSched, betaProbers, betaFactory := concPeerWiring(t, paths, pskB, 0x0BEEF, clk)
211		if err := m.AddConcentratorPeer("beta", pskB, betaSched, betaProbers, betaFactory); err != nil {
212			t.Fatalf("AddConcentratorPeer: %v", err)
213		}
214		if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
215			t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
216		}
217		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
218	
219		beta := m.peersByName["beta"]
220		if beta == nil {
221			t.Fatal("beta peer not registered")
222		}
223		// A concentrator peer's resequencer is lazily built on its first bound PROBE; instantiate it
224		// eagerly here so the test drives its receive plane deterministically.
225		m.ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated(beta)
226		return m, beta
227	}

index 2edc69d..f1850dc 100644
--- a/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go
+++ b/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go
@@ -798,3 +798,339 @@ func TestRebaselineDiscardsBufferedPreSwitchFrames(t *testing.T) {
 		t.Fatalf("released FIFO after Rebaseline = %v, want [0] (prior deliveries must be untouched)", got)

 }
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowSurvivesStaleHighStragglerRace is the reseq-level D36 repro (plan
+// review R126). Under the saturation precondition the plain Rebaseline (unpin + trust
+// the NEXT frame) loses a race: after a PEER RESTART re-baseline, a stale HIGH-seq
+// straggler still draining from the OLD boot can land BEFORE the restarted low-seq init
+// and re-pin `next` HIGH, and with once-per-epoch restart dedup recovery is then blocked.
+// RebaselineToLow re-anchors ONLY on a frame more than one window below the pre-rebaseline
+// release point, so the stale-high straggler is SUSPECT-dropped and the subsequent low
+// init still admits. The parallel with plain Rebaseline is deliberate: swap RebaselineToLow
+// for Rebaseline here and the straggler re-pins next high, the low init is dropped, and the
+// final delivery assertion fails.
+func TestRebaselineToLowSurvivesStaleHighStragglerRace(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Prior boot: a busy contiguous stream advances the release point well past one
+	// window (next == priorHi afterwards).
+	const priorHi = 200
+	for s := uint64(0); s < priorHi; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r)
+
+	// Authenticated peer restart: the low-anchor re-baseline pins the pending anchor at
+	// the current (high) release point rather than unpinning outright.
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.Rebaselines != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d, want 1", s.Rebaselines)
+	}
+
+	// The re-pin RACE: a stale OLD-boot HIGH-seq straggler arrives FIRST. It must be
+	// SUSPECT-dropped and must NOT re-pin `next` high (which a plain Rebaseline would
+	// let it do, blocking recovery).
+	const staleHigh = priorHi + 5 // above the old release point: a genuine "high" straggler
+	beforeSuspect := r.Stats().DroppedSuspect

+// TestRebaselineToLowSmallAnchorSelfHeals is the FIX-1 regression (round-2 review). A
+// RebaselineToLow at a SMALL release point (next <= window) must NOT arm the low-anchor
+// gate: the re-anchor predicate `seq < anchor && anchor - seq > window` is UNSATISFIABLE

+// pin would blackhole the whole restarted stream FOREVER — a regression from the pre-T119
+// resync self-heal. Realistic trigger: light traffic / an early restart / a crash-loop

+// TestRebaselineToLowThenRebaselineDelivers is the FIX-2 regression (round-2 review). A
+// plain Rebaseline() (the D32 hub-failover path) must CLEAR a pending low-anchor left by a
+// prior RebaselineToLow whose restarted low init has not yet re-anchored next. Without the
+// fix, Rebaseline resets `started` but leaves pendingLow armed against the stale
+// pendingLowAnchor, so every post-failover fail-back frame is re-classified against that
+// stale anchor and SUSPECT-dropped — violating Rebaseline's "next Observe re-anchors next"
+// postcondition. The fix clears pendingLow, restoring the plain unpin-and-trust-next path.
+func TestRebaselineToLowThenRebaselineDelivers(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)

+	// Prior boot advances the release point well past one window: next == 200.
+	const priorHi = 200
+	for s := uint64(0); s < priorHi; s++ {

+	// pending low-anchor so the standby's fail-back stream is not gated against the stale
+	// anchor.

+	// these were all SUSPECT-dropped (0/100 delivered) against the stale pendingLowAnchor=200;
+	// after the fix the first frame re-anchors next and the stream DELIVERS in order.

+// isContiguousAscending reports whether s is a strictly +1 ascending run.

+	}

+// (review R150). At release point next == window+1 the low-anchor gate is NOT armed:
+// the re-anchor predicate `seq < anchor && anchor - seq > window` requires
+// anchor >= window+2 to admit any positive seq, so at anchor == window+1 it is
+// unsatisfiable and arming would blackhole. RebaselineToLow must fall back to a PLAIN
+// unpin here, so the restarted stream re-anchors on its first frame with no suspect
+// drops — exactly the D32 hub-failover behaviour.
+func TestRebaselineToLowPlainUnpinAtWindowPlusOne(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Advance the release point to EXACTLY window+1 (observe seqs 0..window, i.e.
+	// window+1 frames, leaves next == window+1).
+	for s := uint64(0); s <= window; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r) // next == window+1
+
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+
+	// Restarted new-boot stream (outer-seq near 1): the plain unpin re-anchors on the
+	// first frame and the stream DELIVERS with zero suspect drops (gate never armed).
+	var want []uint64
+	for s := uint64(1); s <= 6; s++ {

+
+// TestRebaselineToLowArmedAtWindowPlusTwoSatisfiedBySeq1 is the round-3 FIX-3 upper
+// boundary (review R150). At release point next == window+2 the gate IS armed and its
+// SOLE in-budget re-anchor frame is seq 1 (anchor - 1 == window+1 > window). A stale
+// HIGH straggler is SUSPECT-dropped; seq 1 then re-anchors and the new boot delivers.
+// This pins the exact boundary the round-2 guard (arm only when next > window+1) opens.
+func TestRebaselineToLowArmedAtWindowPlusTwoSatisfiedBySeq1(t *testing.T) {

+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)

+	// Advance to EXACTLY window+2 (observe seqs 0..window+1 == window+2 frames).
+	for s := uint64(0); s <= window+1; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}

+	r.Observe(1, payloadOf(1), testSrc)
+	r.Observe(2, payloadOf(2), testSrc)

+		t.Fatalf("seq 1 did not re-anchor the window+2 gate: delivered %v, want [1 2]", got)
+	}

+// TestRebaselineToLowGateRecoversWhenInitFrameLost is the round-3 FIX-3 core regression
+// (fable probe, reproduced 0/499; review R150). At anchor == window+2 the only in-budget
+// re-anchor frame is seq 1. If that lone wrapped-init frame is LOST — loss under
+// saturation is the D36 premise — every later new-boot frame fails `anchor - seq > window`
+// and, on the round-2 logic, is SUSPECT-dropped FOREVER (a permanent blackhole: 0/N
+// delivered). The bounded gate falls back to a plain unpin after O(window) drops, so the
+// new-boot stream self-heals and DELIVERS its tail instead of blackholing.
+func TestRebaselineToLowGateRecoversWhenInitFrameLost(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Advance to EXACTLY window+2 so the gate arms with seq 1 as the sole re-anchor.
+	for s := uint64(0); s <= window+1; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r) // next == window+2
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+
+	// seq 1 is LOST. The restarted stream continues at 2,3,...,last. Each fails the
+	// re-anchor predicate and is SUSPECT-dropped until the bounded gate falls back.
+	const last = 3 * window
+	for s := uint64(2); s <= last; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	got := drain(r)
+
+	if len(got) == 0 {
+		t.Fatal("init frame lost blackholed the whole new-boot stream (0/N) — bounded gate did not fall back")
+	}
+	if !isContiguousAscending(got) {
+		t.Fatalf("recovered delivery is not a contiguous run: %v", got)
+	}
+	if got[len(got)-1] != last {
+		t.Fatalf("recovered stream did not catch up: last delivered %d, want %d", got[len(got)-1], last)
+	}
+	if got[0] <= 1 {

+	}

+	}
